title: Zsofia Barany discussion of: Pay transparency
abstract: The drivers of the gender wage gap and policies that could help reduce it are key issues and are widely researched. This paper analyses whether, when access to justice in cases of pay discrimination is easy, pay transparency can help reduce the unexplained component of the gender gap. On the one hand, pay transparency helps establish whether the pay workers’ receive is fair, and if access to justice is easy, workers could rectify any discrimination and thereby close gender gaps. This direct mechanism can lead to smaller gender gaps ex ante, as employers want to avoid the costs of litigation. On the other hand, pay transparency can lead to resentful employees, which can result in lower productivity and higher turnover. It can also lead to increased litigation, and providing transparent information about pay within a company can be costly. Therefore, the overall effect of these policies can in general be ambiguous.
summary_en: Does pay transparency reduce the gender wage gap? Bárány provides a discussion of an Economic Policy paper analysing this question. The author argues that when access to justice for pay discrimination is easy, pay transparency can help reduce the unexplained component of the gender wage gap by enabling workers to identify and rectify discrimination. But pay transparency also has costs: it can generate resentful employees, reduce productivity, raise turnover, and increase litigation. The overall welfare effect is therefore ambiguous. Bárány's discussion sharpens the policy implications and raises specific questions about implementation in different national legal contexts.
data_used: Discussion paper. No new empirical contribution; engages with the underlying authors' theoretical and empirical analysis of pay-transparency effects.
policy_relevance: For Hungarian gender-equality policy (EBH, NGM): the paper informs the EU Pay Transparency Directive (2023) implementation in Hungary. The trade-off — direct transparency benefit vs. resentful-worker costs — is operationally relevant. For Hungarian companies subject to the directive, the paper supports pairing transparency rules with effective grievance mechanisms (without which transparency may underperform). Caveat: discussion paper; specific Hungarian implementation requires legal-and-institutional analysis.
